ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Shan-style rice fish. FOODON_00002809 (fish dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Shan-style rice fish as a composite dish. A0CKP (Fish, cooked) used as closest defensible parent for the primary protein component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Shan-style rice fish dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of cooked tomato rice and 150 g of boiled fish, as typically plated in a restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Cooked white rice (SR Legacy #20045) and Cooked fish fillet (Foundation Foods, tilapia/freshwater fish, #174203), with tomato sauce component estimated from USDA SR Legacy tomato-based sauce (#11529)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Shan Style Rice Fish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values estimated as a weighted composite: approximately 57% cooked white rice, 43% boiled freshwater fish (tilapia proxy), with minor tomato and aromatics contribution. Per-100g values derived from weighted blend of USDA SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect fish sauce seasoning typical of Shan cuisine.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; white rice (boiled) GI reference value used as primary analogous anchor
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Shan Style Rice Fish. GI estimated at 64 based on boiled white rice GI of 64–72 (mean ~68, glucose reference) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The fish and tomato components contribute negligible glycemic load; the dominant glycemic driver is the white rice portion (~57% of dish by weight). GI of 64 selected as conservative lower bound reflecting the diluting effect of protein and fat from the fish component on overall glycemic response. GL per serving = (64 × 17.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 350g serving / 100) / 100 = approximately 20.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 350 g serving provides approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or consume smaller portions to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has an estimated GI of 64 (medium) with a glycemic load of approximately 20 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider portion control, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, or substituting brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat with lean fish protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (~0.4 g per 100g) and provides lean fish protein, making it a heart-friendly option; however, the sodium content from fish sauce warrants monitoring for individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shan Style Rice Fish is a dish originating from the Shan people of Myanmar and northern Thailand, consisting of tomato-seasoned rice served with gently boiled or poached freshwater fish. The rice is typically cooked with tomatoes, garlic, shallots, lemongrass, and fish sauce, giving it a mildly tangy and savoury flavour profile. The fish — commonly a freshwater variety such as tilapia or carp — is boiled until tender and served alongside or atop the rice. The texture of the dish is soft and moist, with the fish flaking easily and the rice absorbing the aromatic tomato broth. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai restaurants as a light, wholesome meal. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density of approximately 118 kcal per 100g, with a balanced macronutrient profile of roughly 8g protein, 1.4g fat, and 17.8g carbohydrate per 100g. The fish component contributes lean protein and vitamin B12, while the tomato base adds vitamin C and lycopene. The primary clinical consideration is the medium glycemic index (~64) driven by white rice, and moderate sodium content (~310 mg per 100g) from fish sauce seasoning. This dish is suitable for individuals seeking a low-fat, high-protein meal, though portion control is advisable for those managing blood glucose or blood pressure.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Shan Style Rice Fish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked white rice and boiled freshwater fish (tilapia proxy), with sodium adjusted for fish sauce. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled white rice) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA source entries used for this composite estimate. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence in the composite estimation and was not available from the primary USDA entries used. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 g per 100g; confirmed as negligible/trace for boiled freshwater fish and plain cooked rice per USDA FoodData Central — confirmed zero by source. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice as the dominant glycemic driver; the fish and tomato components are assumed to have negligible glycemic contribution. 7. Sodium of 310 mg per 100g reflects an upward adjustment from plain rice and fish values to account for fish sauce seasoning typical of Shan cuisine; this is an estimate and actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 8. The cuisine tag in the input includes 'Salad', 'BBQ', and 'Fried Rice Vermicelli' which appear to be restaurant-level tags from field survey and are not representative of this specific dish; these have been disregarded. 9. Thai mandatory local-language name (ข้าวปลาสไตล์ฉาน) is a descriptive Thai rendering; no single canonical Thai name for this Shan dish was identified in approved sources. 10. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA component data for the constituent ingredients.
